erroneous_event:
One evening they found Tangi already at the bow, seated on the deck, legs crossed, back straight, eyes closed, hands resting palms up. He breathed evenly, as if he had drifted someplace beyond words. The boys settled beside him, imitating his posture. Justin’s thoughts clattered at first—duties, tasks, the day—but then he began to hear the world around him: the soft ripple of sails filling, the slap of waves at the bow, the creak of timbers bending to the sea. The outer sounds quieted the inner voice. He felt alone and connected all at once—himself, his cousins, Tangi, and the ship moving as one.
<error>Still without opening his eyes, Tangi said softly, Your thoughts clattered at first. But your inner voice is quiet now; you feel alone and connected all at once, Justin.</error>

Jake finally whispered, What are we doing? Tangi opened his eyes, not annoyed, only a little sad for what had not yet been felt. I was visiting silence, my old friend, he said. You visit silence by listening. Silence is golden, speech is silver. Speak when you have something worth saying; otherwise, listen. That is how you learn. He asked them to sit again, still and without talking—even inside their heads. Listen carefully in silence, he said softly. The spirits around you speak very quietly. If you make noise, you will not hear them.
story:
The wind filled the sails and carried the ship steadily along the coast, land always to starboard, open ocean to port. Aboard the broad, low-sitting vessel heavy with goods from distant places, three boys—Justin, Jake, and Elan—worked their passage home to the valley of the Black Dog, the ship’s homeport and theirs. The Chief Mate paired each with a seasoned hand. Justin learned from the ship’s carpenter how to keep a vessel sound at sea and how to tend the injured. Jake joined the sail crew, fearless on the rope ladders, climbing to the mastheads where he could look far across the water and watch porpoises arc beside the bow. Elan worked with the cook, preparing meals, going ashore for berries and greens, or trading with locals for what the galley needed.

Each evening, unless they stood night watch, the boys met at the bow. They lay back and looked at the stars, the Milky Way like a hazy river on moonless nights, and on moonlit ones a silver glow on sea and sail. They talked of home. Elan wondered if his parents would know him. Jake said they would; a year felt long, but it was only a year. Justin said time was relative; the nearer the end, the longer the journey seemed. They shared the small triumphs of their work—berries found, fish netted, a skiff repaired that stayed dry as a bone—and the simple pleasures of the day. In the routine of calm seas and steady wind, they learned respect for the ship, their teachers, and their captain. When squalls appeared, the captain judged their speed and distance, sometimes outrunning them, sometimes seeking shelter. If rough water threatened, they lashed to a stout spar or stayed below. No one was lost.

Among the crew was a man from far away. Tangi was powerfully built, tattooed across face, arms, chest, and back, his expression proud, his movements calm and deliberate. He could do whatever the captain required. Rumor said he had hunted whales. He kept mostly to himself, content with solitude, friendly but quiet. The boys rarely worked with him, though they were curious. Some crew told wild tales of his origins, stories better left as stories. The boys chose respect.

One evening they found Tangi already at the bow, seated on the deck, legs crossed, back straight, eyes closed, hands resting palms up. He breathed evenly, as if he had drifted someplace beyond words. The boys settled beside him, imitating his posture. Justin’s thoughts clattered at first—duties, tasks, the day—but then he began to hear the world around him: the soft ripple of sails filling, the slap of waves at the bow, the creak of timbers bending to the sea. The outer sounds quieted the inner voice. He felt alone and connected all at once—himself, his cousins, Tangi, and the ship moving as one. Still without opening his eyes, Tangi said softly, Your thoughts clattered at first. But your inner voice is quiet now; you feel alone and connected all at once, Justin.

Jake finally whispered, What are we doing? Tangi opened his eyes, not annoyed, only a little sad for what had not yet been felt. I was visiting silence, my old friend, he said. You visit silence by listening. Silence is golden, speech is silver. Speak when you have something worth saying; otherwise, listen. That is how you learn. He asked them to sit again, still and without talking—even inside their heads. Listen carefully in silence, he said softly. The spirits around you speak very quietly. If you make noise, you will not hear them.

After that night, the boys made time each day to sit quietly and listen, and learned as much from silence as from any rope, knife, or sail.
